title: Colcannon
subtitle: Irish mashed potato with kale or cabbage and spring onions
excerpt: Colcannon is Irish mashed potato with shredded kale or cabbage and spring onions folded through it, finished with a well of melted butter in the centre. The potato is mashed as smooth as for any mash; the greens are cooked separately and stirred in at the end so they stay identifiable rather than disappearing into the potato. It is a substantial, satisfying dish that pairs with most braises and roasts.
sourceNotes: Traditional Irish dish of mashed potatoes with greens. First documented in print in the eighteenth century; a standard of Irish domestic cookery for centuries. The name comes from the Irish cal ceannann, meaning white-headed cabbage. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): colcannon method.

content.text: Kale and curly kale are the most common green in contemporary colcannon; Savoy cabbage and white cabbage both work well and are more traditional. Whatever green is used, it must be cooked separately and drained thoroughly before folding into the potato: wet greens turn the mash grey and watery. The
in the centre is not decorative; it is part of the dish, providing a sauce to stir through each portion.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled and cut into even chunks

items.name: Spring onion
items.prepNote: finely sliced, white and green parts

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: 60 g for the mash, 20 g for the butter well

content.text: Place the {{potato}} potato chunks in a large saucepan with cold salted water. Bring to the boil and cook for 18 to 20 minutes until completely tender. Drain and return to the dry pan. Leave over a very low heat for 2 minutes to steam-dry.

While the potatoes cook, bring a separate pan of salted water to the boil. Add the shredded {{kale}} and cook for 3 to 4 minutes until just tender. Drain thoroughly and squeeze out as much water as possible.

Mash the potatoes with 60 g of {{unsalted-butter}} and {{whole-milk}} of warm milk until smooth. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Fold in the drained kale and {{spring-onion}} spring onions. Taste and adjust seasoning. Transfer to a warm serving dish. Make a well in the centre and place the remaining 20 g of cold butter in it. Serve immediately.

rows.fix: Squeeze the drained kale in a clean cloth or push it through the colander with the back of a spoon. Every bit of excess water should be removed before it goes into the mash.

content.text: Colcannon is a classic of Irish home cooking. It is linked to Halloween, when a ring or coin was hidden inside for luck. It turns up on British tables wherever there is an Irish connection, and it has become a common stand-in for plain mash in autumn and winter. Its close relative, champ, uses spring onions and milk but no greens.
